summary:
The Qing Dynasty (1644–1912 AD) was the last imperial dynasty of China, established by the Manchu ethnic minority who conquered the crumbling [[event:2374|Ming Dynasty]] in 1644 and ruled one of the largest empires in history, at its 18th-century peak encompassing roughly 13 million square kilometers and 300 million people — representing approximately one-third of global GDP. The Qing rulers faced the perpetual challenge of ruling a Han Chinese majority population as an ethnic minority: they maintained a dual administrative structure (Manchu and Chinese officials), required Manchu nobles to maintain traditional warrior culture while adopting Chinese governance practices, and enforced the notorious queue order (forcing Han men to adopt the Manchu hairstyle — shaved front with a braid — under pain of death, symbolizing submission). The [[event:3294|Kangxi]], [[event:2463|Qianlong]], and Yongzheng emperors of the 18th century presided over a period of expansion and prosperity often called the 'High Qing.'

The [[event:3299|Ten Great Campaigns]] of the Qianlong Emperor (r. 1736–1796) extended Qing territory to its maximum extent, incorporating Tibet, Xinjiang, and Mongolia, creating what remains essentially the territory of modern China. The [[event:2444|Treaty of Nerchinsk]] (1689) with Russia defined the northern border. The [[event:3244|Canton System]] restricted Western trade to the single port of Guangzhou, and the [[event:3300|Macartney Mission]] (1793) famously failed to open additional trade with Britain. The Qing dynasty's decline began with the [[event:3245|White Lotus Rebellion]] (1796–1804), continued through the catastrophic [[event:3298|Dzungar-Qing Wars]] and internal rebellions, and accelerated dramatically with British military superiority in the Opium Wars (1839–1842, 1856–1860) — a humiliation that exposed the dynasty's structural decay before its final collapse in 1912.
